devices.name: JX-3P
devices.notes: Often sold as a generic 80s keyboard; PG-200 programmer (if included) adds value.

devices.name: JX-8P
devices.notes: Interface looks 'digital' so some sellers undervalue it; programmer/controller inclusion matters.

devices.name: Alpha Juno 1
devices.notes: Mispriced because of the single-slider UI; 'hoover' association drives demand.

devices.name: Alpha Juno 2
devices.notes: Often sold by non-synth folks as a 'MIDI keyboard'; condition of keybed matters.

devices.name: MKS-50
devices.notes: Rack gear is regularly undervalued; programmer (PG-300) boosts usability/value.

devices.name: Poly-61
devices.notes: Often bundled with stands/cases; sellers may not know it's an analog poly.

devices.name: Poly-800
devices.notes: Not always a €1000-class item, but deals happen; common and easy to resell.

devices.name: DW-6000
devices.notes: Often assumed to be 'digital junk'; hybrid character is the value.

devices.name: DW-8000
devices.notes: Built-in delay is a selling point; frequently undervalued in local listings.

devices.name: AX-60
devices.notes: Akai branding makes some sellers assume 'sampler company' rather than vintage synth value.

devices.name: AX-73
devices.notes: Less famous than peers; occasionally priced like a generic controller.

devices.name: VX-600
devices.notes: Rarity can reduce mispricing frequency, but occasional uninformed listings exist.

devices.name: SH-101
devices.notes: Usually priced correctly; bargains happen in local cash sales or incomplete listings.

devices.name: SH-09
devices.notes: Less famous than SH-101; some sellers undervalue if they don’t track synth markets.

devices.name: SH-2
devices.notes: Typically expensive; true steals are rare, but worth watching.

devices.name: MS-20
devices.notes: Usually priced correctly; bargains more likely if listed without clear photos/model. Distinguish from MS-20 Mini reissue (2013, worth only ~€300–400).

devices.name: Mono/Poly
devices.notes: Confusion over mono/poly nature sometimes lowers perceived value.

devices.name: CS-10
devices.notes: Overshadowed by higher CS models; can be undervalued outside synth circles.

devices.name: CS-15
devices.notes: Sellers sometimes treat it as 'old Yamaha keyboard' rather than a real analog synth.

devices.name: MKS-30
devices.notes: If listed simply as a 'Roland rack module', can slip under market.

devices.name: MKS-70
devices.notes: Sometimes sold by studios liquidating rack gear; programmer/internals vary by revision.

devices.name: MKS-80
devices.notes: Usually priced correctly; include mainly as a long-shot watch item.

devices.name: Matrix-1000
devices.notes: Rack-module undervaluation effect; some sellers don’t know Oberheim cachet.

devices.name: ESQ-1
devices.architecture: digital oscillators with analog filter + sequencer
devices.notes: Huge value gap because it looks like a 'workstation'. Great candidate for €300–€500 finds.

devices.name: SQ-80
devices.architecture: digital oscillators with analog filter + sequencer
devices.notes: Often priced like a generic workstation; cult following keeps resale strong.

devices.name: K3
devices.notes: Underrated; sellers often assume it's a low-value digital keyboard.

devices.name: TR-505
devices.notes: Not usually a €1000 item, but can be a cheap add-on flip when bundled.

devices.name: TR-626
devices.notes: Often confused with low-end drum boxes; value varies by condition.

devices.name: TR-707
devices.notes: Solid €200–€400 bargain potential when sellers don’t track vintage drum prices.

devices.name: TR-727
devices.notes: Often misunderstood as a niche percussion box; still valuable to collectors/producers.

devices.name: S950
devices.notes: Classic cult sampler; rack gear + 'obsolete' perception creates frequent undervaluation.

devices.name: S900
devices.notes: Often sold cheap when untested; good candidate if you can verify it boots and loads.

devices.name: SDS-V (modules)
devices.notes: Sometimes sold as incomplete electronic drum junk; modules/brain/pads split listings are opportunities.

devices.name: SDS-9
devices.notes: Most common Simmons on the secondhand market; often sold incomplete (brain only or pads only) at low prices.

devices.name: MS-10
devices.notes: Occasionally undervalued when listed without model clarity; smaller than MS-20 so easier logistics.

devices.name: Axxe
devices.notes: True steals are rare; more likely via estate/studio clearance with poor listing details.

devices.name: Odyssey (early versions)
devices.notes: Usually priced correctly; keep as a high-upside watch item for badly titled listings.

devices.name: Pianet T
devices.notes: Smaller and lighter than Rhodes; often misidentified by non-musicians.

devices.name: Pianet N
devices.notes: Used by Beatles and others; sometimes sold as a generic organ.

devices.name: Stage 73
devices.notes: Shipping weight causes some sellers to list cheaply for local pickup.

devices.name: Suitcase 73
devices.notes: Large footprint often leads to quick-sale pricing.

devices.name: MK I Stage 88
devices.notes: Heavy; sellers sometimes accept lower local prices.

devices.name: SM7
devices.notes: Predecessor of SM7B; some sellers don't know the value difference.

devices.name: RE20
devices.notes: Very liquid resale market; common in studio closures.

devices.name: MD 421
devices.notes: Vintage white versions especially valuable.

devices.name: U87 (early models)
devices.notes: Rarely mispriced but extremely profitable if found under market.

devices.name: RE-201 Space Echo
devices.notes: High demand; bargains mostly appear as 'untested' or poorly titled local-pickup listings.

devices.name: RE-301 Chorus Echo
devices.architecture: analog tape delay + spring reverb + chorus
devices.notes: Often valued as 'like a Space Echo but better' by buyers; sellers may not know the model difference.

devices.name: Echocord Mini / Super (tape echo units)
devices.notes: German market sometimes has local underpricing; watch for 'works but needs service' listings.

devices.name: EP-3 Echoplex
devices.notes: Usually priced correctly; steals mostly happen when listed vaguely as 'tape echo' with poor photos.

devices.name: Deluxe Memory Man (big box, vintage)
devices.notes: Sellers sometimes price like a generic delay; vintage big-box versions have collector pull.

devices.name: Electric Mistress (vintage, big box)
devices.notes: Big-box form factor can deter casual buyers; creates occasional bargains.

devices.name: Phase 90 (script logo era)
devices.notes: ‘Script’ vs later versions is often missed in listings; photos are key.

devices.name: Distortion+ (vintage)
devices.notes: Often bundled with other pedals; good target in lot listings.

devices.name: PCM 41
devices.notes: Rack gear undervaluation is real; listings titled only 'delay unit' can be underpriced.

devices.name: PCM 70
devices.notes: Very liquid; some sellers treat it like any old rack processor.

devices.name: H3000 (various revisions)
devices.notes: Steals happen when listed generically as 'harmonizer' with no model; check front panel photos.

devices.name: SDE-1000 / SDE-2000
devices.notes: Good underpricing frequency because many buyers chase only tape echos; these are often 'cheap rack delays' to sellers.

devices.name: Polysix
devices.architecture: VCO analog subtractive with built-in chorus
devices.notes: Famous for warm chorus; battery leakage is common, so 'defective' units can be great deals if only the battery needs replacing.

devices.name: Juno-6
devices.architecture: DCO analog subtractive with built-in chorus
devices.notes: No MIDI makes some sellers heavily discount it; the built-in chorus is iconic. Often confused with cheaper Juno models.

devices.name: Juno-60
devices.architecture: DCO analog subtractive with built-in chorus
devices.notes: Usually priced correctly but occasionally appears in estate/studio clearance listings; MIDI retrofit adds value.

devices.name: Prophet-600
devices.notes: GliGli firmware upgrade increases desirability; sometimes sold without knowing the Sequential pedigree.

devices.name: Six-Trak
devices.architecture: VCO analog subtractive with multi-timbral sequencer
devices.notes: Built-in multi-timbral sequencer is underappreciated; budget Sequential is a real deal magnet.

devices.name: Prodigy
devices.notes: Small size can mislead sellers about value; Moog brand always has strong resale.

devices.name: Rogue
devices.notes: Cheapest real Moog; looks toy-like so often priced as such.

devices.name: Source
devices.architecture: VCO analog subtractive with digital membrane controls
devices.notes: Membrane panel confuses sellers into thinking it's 'broken digital'; actually fully analog signal path.

devices.name: DR-110 (Dr. Rhythm Graphic)
devices.notes: Tiny and plastic-looking; sellers often list for €50–100 not knowing it's a real analog drum machine.

devices.name: Drumulator
devices.notes: Lo-fi digital sound is prized in hip-hop and electronic; often sold as broken/unknown drum machine.

devices.name: DMX
devices.notes: Hip-hop legend; 'DMX' name collision with rapper causes search noise but also means listings get missed.

devices.name: LinnDrum
devices.notes: Usually priced correctly; long-shot watch item for estate/studio clearances.

devices.name: 200A
devices.architecture: reed electric piano with built-in amplifier/speaker
devices.notes: Possibly the single best deal target on Kleinanzeigen. Commonly listed as 'altes E-Piano' or 'Wurlitzer Klavier' by non-musicians. Heavy, which drives local-pickup bargains.

devices.name: Clavinet D6
devices.notes: Iconic Stevie Wonder / funk sound. Sellers sometimes confuse with generic Hohner home keyboards.

devices.name: CE-1 Chorus Ensemble
devices.notes: First Boss pedal ever made; large size makes some sellers price it as a generic old effect.

devices.name: DM-2 Delay
devices.notes: Common in pedal lot listings; sellers often don't distinguish between analog and digital Boss delays.

devices.name: SDD-320 Dimension D
devices.notes: Cult studio classic; generic 'rack chorus' or 'Rackeffekt' listings are the typical deal opportunity.

devices.name: Mu-Tron III
devices.notes: Very rare in Europe; long-shot item but enormous upside if found. Jerry Garcia / Bootsy Collins association.
